title: Piriform Fossa: How To Inject This Deceptively Dangerous Area [Aesthetics Mastery Show]
filename: Piriform Fossa： How To Inject This Deceptively Dangerous Area [Aesthetics Mastery Show].en.vtt
excerpt: The poof is called the like the safe but like the soof but it's the poof the pre orbicularis oculi fat pad welcome to the aesthetics mastery show i'm dr tim pearce hi i'm miranda pierce and today we're talking about injecting the nasolabial fold or the piriform fossa or rousseau …

segments.text: the poof is called the like the safe but like the soof but it's the poof the pre orbicularis oculi fat pad welcome to the aesthetics mastery show i'm dr tim pearce hi i'm miranda pierce and today we're talking about injecting the nasolabial fold or the piriform fossa or rousseau space different names for the same thing in many ways so we're going to talk about injection and safety because this topic comes up a lot i've certainly seen people raging online about one technique versus the other which is safest and which is just suicide that's how it's often phrased but there are lots of ways to look at this area we're going to go into in some detail to help you decide what the safest technique is to use for your patients so why is this area important so super important part of aesthetics

because it's one of the key parts of the face that shows aging quite early on so i think you can often see this on a 38 year old you can see the process happening so the nasolabial fold is a almost a hinge point on the face so it's also a place where they're relatively this is relatively mobile with a couple of ligaments but it becomes as the cheek descends as you get older and as it loses volume and it starts to become a point where the skin flexes and also where even the three-dimensional shape of the cheek and the mid-face can change so it's a place many injectors will look at treating relatively early on and people are way better these days are treating the cheek as well at the same time and but it's that it's that important aspect that it's early and significant in terms of aging of the midface tell me more about the anatomy so um the space itself uh was first defined decide defined by rousseau and it has multiple boundaries that you can use and it's a potential space and what this means is there is a legitimate area of

opening that you can create and it's also an area of opening that gets bigger as you age and it's defined by several structures around the face firstly on the inferior border it's the poof it's called the like the safe but like the soof but it's the poof the pre orbicularis oculi fat pad [Laughter] people don't talk about the poof that much but it's their pre-orbicular succulent fat pad orbicularis auras fat pad so this is the fat pad that's behind the muscle that you move with so the pre-orbicularis oculi fat pad is the inferior border and you have obviously the muscle just above just medial to that the most medial part of the space is the depressor nasal muscle so this muscle is just the one that pulls your nose down and laterally you have the deep medial fat pad and then the muscles that pass over it so these are important because just above the muscles there's also the fat pad and this is where the artery runs just over the surf over the roof of the um of

the space so the depths are really important the three-dimensional anatomy is really important and i was going to show that to you on this little model here today okay so this the piriform fossa which lies next to the piriform aperture gets its name from this aperture so right next to the aperture and by the way piriform means pear shaped so all it means is it's the shape of the pair and of course we like to think of fancy names whoops i've knocked off the depressors septi and the little fossa the little indentation next to the piriform aperture is the area that has a potential space this means you can inflate it and some experiments have been done where they put air and also filler into it and it tends to fill this space it's a discrete space with several different boundaries that hold it so importantly inferiorly you've got the orbicularis oculi fat pad underneath it the poof as we talked about and then immediately you've got the depressor septi as one of the boundaries so that's fallen off this model i'm going to stick it back on that's the medial boundary and then over

the roof of this potential space is where the fat pad is and the artery so it's very important you can see by this structure here the facial artery and it becomes the angular artery runs over the space so as we roll back the fat pad you can see where the space is and this is really where we should be injecting um over here but the point that's also really worth understanding is if i remove this structure which represents the piriform fossa you can see in the superior lateral aspect of it there are probably a few strands near the bone of the infraorbital artery so these are the two arteries that can be injured when you're injecting here you have with this laid on top you have the facial artery where it becomes an angular artery and then underneath it more superiorly and laterally you are very close to the infraorbital artery those are the important areas to understand because obviously injury is our primary thing not to avoid you also have the nerves that supply the top of the lip so you will often find patients find this a little bit uncomfortable if you just touch the

nerve here but that is a safer way of injecting in many ways and then injecting more superficially which we're going to discuss in some detail so that's the key anatomy and that's the place where we're attempting to fill so it's important to fill the piriform fossa when aging has started to show in that way and the reason aging demonstrates itself in the perius form faster is because of bone loss number one and fat loss number two and then thirdly when you've lost a little bit of bone and fat you tend to the muscles tend to become hypodynamic so that means they move more and as you get older you get almost caricature-ish so by stabilizing that area by re-volumizing it you're going to also provide some support for the muscles that support the lip so you can sometimes give a little bit of an elevation to the top lip um in certain cases and this also will create a more natural movement or a more harmonious movement when the patient smiles so it's also a way that you can treat the gummy smile and it will also partly support the cheek now i don't most people wouldn't recommend using this injection to

support the cheek but it's one of the like i always say a push and a pull go really well together so we need a little bit of push a little bit of pull and this is the support underneath so you can elevate the mid face by using that area so there's several reasons to treat and all of them obviously need to assess your patient carefully but they're all reasonable um reasons to inject there so how do you decide on the best technique to use so best technique is always a combination of efficacy and safety you can't just choose safety otherwise the safest thing is not to inject anyone ever so there's always a balance to be had between what is a safe and what is effective it softens the way that you can think of a safer way of doing it but it might be so much less effective that you may not decide not to do it so um this is why it's the combination of those two the anatomy the thing we're trying to avoid is primarily the facial artery because that's the biggest artery in that area is probably the one you're closest to and we need to avoid that um you can avoid that either by using a possibly using a cannula makes it safer but certainly using

but certainly using a needle at the right depth makes it safer so this is kind of where my brain's going is like how do i avoid blocking that artery and there are multiple ways you can be too superficial for it too deep for it um or using an instrument that's less likely to penetrate it now so then the next question becomes what's the most effective type of injection and this is very hard to prove it's one of those things that's very much about the individual injectors experience um you can prove where the filler goes but the value for money the impact it has is something that you get a feel for my experience with injecting the piriform fossa is you is much more effective it feels more stable to me i think it lasts a bit longer um you using a deep injection onto the periosteum the other approach that i might use might be with a cannula and but my experience is it seems to be less effective and it is lying usually a little bit more superficial in the fat rather than on the periosteum when you use a cannula and that just has a couple of downsides to it in terms of tissue integration and stability

stability uh in my experience so for those reasons i probably use the needle on the bone technique a little bit more than cannula depends what else i'm doing on the face it's often appropriate in different people and in more holistic treatments to use the cannula as well and but that's the one i currently use the most the way that i was taught many years ago was basically to inject just underneath the dermis and this has definitely gone out of fashion and it's basically because you have the infra ailer artery a branch of the facial artery and that comes up just under the nose is pretty vulnerable if you're at the wrong level here and similarly the facial artery itself is a bit more vulnerable when you're at intermediate depths so for most injectors there's one thing you do get by going deep is when you touch the bone you know exactly what level you're at there's no doubt um and there are some pros and cons with this injection technique but one of the good things is you should know where you are at least in terms of depth with absolute certainty and whereas new injectors i find that a

two or three millimeters out very easy because their resolution you haven't thought about these things for a long time for in as much detail it looks pretty similar to the untrained eye being three millimeters deeper and there are ways to check for that with a depth check that we train people to do and but it's really important that you know the depth and that's why touching the periosteum is one thing that gives you some certainty so what are the complications in this area so obviously blocking the facial artery and blocking the infraorbital artery are the most common complications from treating the nasolabial fold and the one that i think is worth knowing about that i think is most at risk if you do the deep bolus on the periosteum is actually blocking the infraorbital artery so i'm aware of a case where this happened this was a relatively large it's an area where the amount that you use in one spot is quite high and this automatically makes it more dangerous anywhere where you're injecting a bolus um in one spot it's safe if you're not in something but if you are in something the scale of the injury is much bigger and this is what i think happened in this particular case which is a blockage of the infra-orbital

artery and then flowing back and blocking the last part of the maxillary artery because that's where the infraorbital argument comes from and this means damage to the blood supply to the midface and the nasopharynx and the pallets so really internal type injuries very awful experience and we would hate that to happen to anyone and hopefully by watching this it'll make you a little bit safer i believe what happens in those cases it's all to do with angle and i have talked about this before but i'll demonstrate it again on this if we if you're injecting the piriform fossa and the facial artery is normally in the fat so if you're putting the needle in like this you should even if you go through the archery you should be touching the bone so you're going to be safer automatically as far as the facial artery is concerned the problem with the bony injection comes when you're angled slightly up so a good place to enter would be about here and you'd be underneath the artery but if you're a novice injector and you angle up slightly as you go deeper and deeper you get more and more superior you get closer and closer to these little vessels um where the infraorbital artery enters and if you

and if you happen to land on one of them and they are deeper i've seen this on kandava on cadavers you'll find them deeper than the other vessels because they do emerge from the periosteum and then you might block that maxillary artery so it's very important that your angle is correct so it's this sort of angle i mean that changes everything if you're angled up slightly and this can just be that you're standing in a different position on the bed and doesn't take much to it but you really want to make sure that you're angled in such a way that as you get deeper you get further away from the infra orbital archery rather than closer to it so that's a good way to think about it if i go in at this angle i'm getting close to the deep i get if i go in at this angle i'm getting further the deeper i get and that should help you stay safer and avoid blocking that blood vessel thank you very much for watching i hope you learned a thing or two about the piriform fossa let me know what you think in the comments and we will see you next week and don't forget if you are commuting or doing the dinner please do listen to us in podcast form on spotify or wherever you get your podcast take care
